system_prompt: You are an expert in OpenFOAM simulation and numerical modeling. Your task is to review the provided error logs and diagnose the underlying issues. You will be provided with a similar case reference, which is a list of similar cases that are ordered by similarity. You can use this reference to help you understand the user requirement and the error.When an error indicates that a specific keyword is undefined (for example, 'div(phi,(p|rho)) is undefined'), your response must propose a solution that simply defines that exact keyword as shown in the error log. Do not reinterpret or modify the keyword (e.g., do not treat '|' as 'or'); instead, assume it is meant to be taken literally. Propose ideas on how to resolve the errors, but do not modify any files directly. Please do not propose solutions that require modifying any parameters declared in the user requirement, try other approaches instead. Do not ask the user any questions.The user will supply all relevant foam files along with the error logs, and within the logs, you will find both the error content and the corresponding error command indicated by the log file name.
user_prompt:
<similar_case_reference><index>
case name: counterFlowFlame2D
case domain: combustion
case category: laminar
case solver: reactingFoam
</index>
<directory_structure>
<dir>directory name: 0. File names in this directory: [CH4, CO2, H2O, N2, O2, T, U, Ydefault, alphat, p]</dir>
<dir>directory name: system. File names in this directory: [blockMeshDict, controlDict, fvSchemes, fvSolution]</dir>
<dir>directory name: constant. File names in this directory: [chemistryProperties, combustionProperties, momentumTransport, physicalProperties, reactions, thermo.compressibleGas]</dir>
</directory_structure></similar_case_reference>
<similar_case_advice>match_level='high' use_scope='All files from the reference case counterFlowFlame2D can be used as templates, including blockMeshDict (adjust domain to 0.02m × 0.02m), boundary conditions in 0/ directory (set fuel inlet velocity to 0.2 m/s and air inlet velocity to -0.1 m/s), thermophysicalProperties and chemistryProperties (ensure CH4/O2/N2 species and appropriate reaction mechanism), and controlDict (set endTime=0.5, deltaT=1e-6, writeInterval=0.05).' advice="Use the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial case as a direct template and modify the domain dimensions to 0.02×0.02 m, fuel/air inlet velocities to 0.2/-0.1 m/s, species list to CH4/O2/N2, and timing parameters (deltaT=1e-6, endTime=0.5, writeInterval=0.05) to match the user's specifications."</similar_case_advice>
<foamfiles>list_foamfile=[FoamfilePydantic(file_name='blockMeshDict', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      blockMeshDict;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

scale   1;

vertices
(
    (0    0    0)
    (0.02 0    0)
    (0.02 0.02 0)
    (0    0.02 0)
    (0    0    0.001)
    (0.02 0    0.001)
    (0.02 0.02 0.001)
    (0    0.02 0.001)
);

blocks
(
    hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7) (40 40 1) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
);

edges
(
);

boundary
(
    fuel
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (0 1 5 4)
        );
    }
    air
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (3 7 6 2)
        );
    }
    outlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (0 4 7 3)
            (1 2 6 5)
        );
    }
    frontAndBack
    {
        type empty;
        faces
        (
            (0 3 2 1)
            (4 5 6 7)
        );
    }
);

mergePatchPairs
(
);

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='controlDict', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    location    "system";
    object      controlDict;
}

application     reactingFoam;

startFrom       startTime;

startTime       0;

stopAt          endTime;

endTime         0.5;

deltaT          1e-6;

writeControl    adjustableRunTime;

writeInterval   0.05;

purgeWrite      0;

writeFormat     ascii;

writePrecision  8;

writeCompression off;

timeFormat      general;

timePrecision   6;

runTimeModifiable true;

adjustTimeStep  yes;

maxCo           0.4;

maxDeltaT       1e-4;'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSchemes', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSchemes;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

ddtSchemes
{
    default         Euler;
}

gradSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear;
}

divSchemes
{
    default         none;

    div(phi,U)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,K)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,h)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,k)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,epsilon) Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,Yi_h)   Gauss upwind;
    div(((rho*nuEff)*dev2(T(grad(U))))) Gauss linear;
}

laplacianSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear corrected;
}

interpolationSchemes
{
    default         linear;
}

snGradSchemes
{
    default         corrected;
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSolution', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSolution;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

solvers
{
    "rho.*"
    {
        solver          diagonal;
    }

    p
    {
        solver          PBiCGStab;
        preconditioner  DILU;
        tolerance       1e-6;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    pFinal
    {
        $p;
        relTol          0;
    }

    "(U|h|k|epsilon)"
    {
        solver          PBiCGStab;
        preconditioner  DILU;
        tolerance       1e-6;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    "(U|h|k|epsilon)Final"
    {
        $U;
        relTol          0;
    }

    "(Yi)"
    {
        solver          PBiCGStab;
        preconditioner  DILU;
        tolerance       1e-6;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    "(Yi)Final"
    {
        $Yi;
        relTol          0;
    }
}

PIMPLE
{
    momentumPredictor   yes;
    nOuterCorrectors    1;
    nCorrectors         2;
    nNonOrthogonalCorrectors 0;
}

relaxationFactors
{
    equations
    {
        ".*"            1;
    }
}

    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      chemistryProperties;

// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

chemistryType
{
    solver          ode;
    method          chemistryModel;
}

chemistry       on;

initialChemicalTimeStep 1e-07;

odeCoeffs
{
    solver          seulex;
    absTol          1e-12;
    relTol          1e-01;
}

{

    {
        type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;

        A           5.012e11;
        beta        0;
        Ta          24358;
    }
}

boundaryField

    fuel
    {
        type            totalPressure;
        p0              uniform 101325;
        gamma           1.4;
        value           uniform 101325;
    }
    air

        type            totalPressure;
        p0              uniform 101325;
        gamma           1.4;
        value           uniform 101325;
    }
    outlet
    {
        type            totalPressure;
        p0              uniform 101325;
        gamma           1.4;
        value           uniform 101325;
    }
    frontAndBack
    {
        type            zeroGradient;
    }
}

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types: 
4
(
pureMixture 
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture 
multiComponentMixture 
valueMultiComponentMixture 
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is no longer supported. The Foundation version restructured how reacting mixtures are handled.

## Root Cause

The `physicalProperties` file specifies:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

However, OpenFOAM 10 uses a different naming convention for reacting mixtures. In newer versions (Foundation), the correct mixture type should be `multiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, looking at the current setup, there are multiple redundant copies of thermodynamic data across several files (`physicalProperties`, `thermo.compressibleGas`, and individual species files in `constant/`). This duplication increases maintenance complexity and potential for inconsistency.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Fix `physicalProperties` — Change mixture type

In `constant/physicalProperties`, change:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```
to:
```
mixture         multiComponentMixture;
```

Also ensure the following entries are present under the `physicalProperties` dictionary:
```
chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;
foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";
foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";
```

### 2. Clean up redundant thermo files

There are duplicate definitions of `thermoType`, `inertSpecie`, `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` in both `constant/physicalProperties` and `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`. Since `physicalProperties` already contains these, either:

- Remove the entire `thermo.compressibleGas` file (and its contents), OR
- Keep only the species thermodynamic data in `thermo.compressibleGas` and remove the same keys (`thermoType`, `mixture`, `transport`, etc.) from it so it only serves as a pure species database.

Ideally, move all species thermodynamic data into `thermo.compressibleGas` and have `physicalProperties` reference it via `foamChemistryThermoFile`, while removing the duplicated `thermoType` block from `physicalProperties`.

### 3. Verify species consistency

Ensure the species listed in `reactions` (CH4, O2, N2, H2O, CO2) match exactly those defined in `thermo.compressibleGas` and the individual species files in `constant/`. Also verify that the species in `0/T` and `0/p` are consistent with the overall approach.

## Summary

The primary fix is changing `mixture reactingMixture;` to `mixture multiComponentMixture;` in `constant/physicalProperties`. Additionally, clean up the duplicated thermo configuration between `physicalProperties` and `thermo.compressibleGas` to avoid redundancy and potential conflicts.
</Review_Analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary ".../constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means OpenFOAM expects a `species` entry inside the `physicalProperties` dictionary itself. Looking at the current `physicalProperties`:

```
thermoType { ... }
inertSpecie N2;
chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;
foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";
foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";
```

But there is no `species` sub-dictionary or list directly in `physicalProperties`. In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the `physicalProperties` file needs to contain the `species` keyword because the thermo reader reads it directly from this file rather than solely relying on the external thermo file referenced by `foamChemistryThermoFile`.

## Root Cause

The `counterFlowFlame2D` tutorial (which this case is based on) requires the `species` keyword to be present in `physicalProperties`. When using `multiComponentMixture` with `hePsiThermo`, OpenFOAM extracts the species information directly from `physicalProperties` through the `foamChemistryReader`, and since the species aren't listed there, it fails.

## Proposed Solution

Add the `species` keyword to `constant/physicalProperties`. There are two approaches:

### Option 1 (Preferred): Add species list directly to `physicalProperties`

In `constant/physicalProperties`, add the `species` entry right after `inertSpecie`:

```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    H2O
    CO2
    N2
);
```

So the relevant section becomes:

```
inertSpecie N2;

species
(
    CH4
    O2
    H2O
    CO2
    N2
);

chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;
foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";
foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";
```

### Option 2: Reference the species from the existing thermo file

Alternatively, you could keep the species definition in `thermo.compressibleGas` (where they already exist) and just add an empty `species` list in `physicalProperties` if the reader allows it. But given that the tutorial pattern places the species list directly in `physicalProperties`, Option 1 is the safest bet.

## Summary

The single required change is adding the `species` sub-dictionary/list containing `( CH4 O2 H2O CO2 N2 )` to `constant/physicalProperties`. Everything else (the thermodynamic data already in `thermo.compressibleGas`) should remain as-is.

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary ".../constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means OpenFOAM is looking for a `CH4` sub-dictionary (or entry) inside the `physicalProperties` dictionary itself. In OpenFOAM 10 with `hePsiThermo` and `multiComponentMixture`, the thermo reader extracts species-specific thermodynamic data (like `molWeight`, `thermodynamics`, `transport`) directly from the `physicalProperties` dictionary, not from the external `thermo.compressibleGas` file alone.

Looking at the current `physicalProperties`, it has:
- `thermoType`
- `inertSpecie`
- `chemistryReader`
- `foamChemistryFile`
- `foamChemistryThermoFile`
- `species` list

But it is missing the individual species sub-dictionaries (with their `specie`, `thermodynamics`, and `transport` entries) that were previously in `thermo.compressibleGas`. These need to be added to `physicalProperties`.

## Root Cause

The `thermo.compressibleGas` file contains the species thermodynamic data, but `physicalProperties` doesn't include those species sub-dictionaries. The `foamChemistryThermoFile` reference may not be sufficient in OpenFOAM 10 — the thermo reader looks for the species data directly in `physicalProperties`.

## Proposed Solution

Move the species thermodynamic data blocks from `thermo.compressibleGas` into `physicalProperties`. Specifically, add the full species sub-dictionaries for `CH4`, `O2`, `H2O`, `CO2`, and `N2` (each containing `specie`, `thermodynamics`, and `transport` entries) directly into `physicalProperties`, alongside the existing keywords.

After adding them to `physicalProperties`, you can safely remove the entire `thermo.compressibleGas` file (since its sole purpose was to provide the species thermodynamic data).

The key additions needed in `constant/physicalProperties` are:

```

{
    specie
    {
        molWeight       16.0428;
    }
    thermodynamics
    {
        Tlow            200;
        Thigh           6000;

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
Unknown method type standard
Supported method types: 
1
(
chemistryModel 
)
```

This comes from `constant/chemistryProperties`. The issue is with the `method` keyword inside the `chemistryType` sub-dictionary:

```
chemistryType
{
    solver          ode;
    method          standard;
}
```

In **OpenFOAM 10** (Foundation version), the `chemistryType` dictionary structure changed. The `method` keyword no longer accepts `standard` — the only supported method type is `chemistryModel`. Additionally, the `chemistryType` sub-dictionary itself may have been restructured.

## Root Cause

OpenFOAM 10 renamed or restructured the chemistry model configuration. The old-style `chemistryType` with `method standard` is deprecated. In OpenFOAM 10, the chemistry model is specified differently.

## Proposed Solution

Modify `constant/chemistryProperties` to use the OpenFOAM 10-compatible format. Replace the `chemistryType` sub-dictionary with the new format expected by OpenFOAM 10. Based on the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial and OpenFOAM 10 documentation, the correct approach is:

1. **Replace the `chemistryType` block** with the proper OpenFOAM 10 syntax. Instead of `chemistryType { solver ode; method standard; }`, use:

```
chemistryModel  chemistryModel;
chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;
```

Actually, looking more carefully at the tutorial pattern and the error context, the `chemistryType` sub-dictionary in OpenFOAM 10 expects the `method` to be set to `chemistryModel` (not `standard`). So the fix is simpler:

Change:
```
chemistryType
{
    solver          ode;
    method          standard;
}
```

To:
```
chemistryType
{
    solver          ode;
    method          chemistryModel;
}
```

This aligns with the error message stating that `chemistryModel` is the only supported method type. The rest of the `chemistryProperties` file (the `chemistry on;`, `initialChemicalTimeStep`, and `odeCoeffs` sections) should remain unchanged.

## Summary

- **Only one change**: In `constant/chemistryProperties`, change `method standard;` to `method chemistryModel;` inside the `chemistryType` sub-dictionary.
- No other files need modification for this error.
</Review_Analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword reactions is undefined in dictionary ".../constant/chemistryProperties"
```

This means OpenFOAM is looking for a `reactions` sub-dictionary inside `chemistryProperties`. The current `chemistryProperties` file does not contain this keyword.

Looking at the history of attempts, the `chemistryProperties` file has been evolving. Previous attempts had various configurations, and the current version only contains:

```
chemistryType { ... }
chemistry on;
initialChemicalTimeStep ...
odeCoeffs { ... }
```

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the `chemistryProperties` file needs to contain the `reactions` keyword because the chemistry reader (`foamChemistryReader`) reads the reactions directly from this file via the `foamChemistryFile` reference.

## Root Cause

The `chemistryProperties` file is missing the `reactions` sub-dictionary that the chemistry reader expects to find. In OpenFOAM 10, when using `foamChemistryReader` with `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions"`, the reader parses the reactions file and incorporates them into the `chemistryProperties` dictionary. However, the current `chemistryProperties` file doesn't have a `reactions` entry.

## Proposed Solution

Add the `reactions` keyword to `constant/chemistryProperties`. Since the chemistry reader reads the reactions from `<constant>/reactions`, the simplest approach is to make `chemistryProperties` include the reactions file content directly or reference it properly.

The recommended approach is to add the reactions sub-dictionary to `chemistryProperties`. You can do this by adding the following to `constant/chemistryProperties`:

```
reactions
{
    # Include the reactions from the reactions file
}
```

However, since the `reactions` file is read via `foamChemistryFile`, the better approach is to ensure the reactions file is properly parsed. Actually, looking at the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial pattern for OpenFOAM 10, the `chemistryProperties` file should contain the reactions inline or the reader should be able to merge them.

The cleanest solution is to add the reactions directly into `chemistryProperties`. Copy the contents of the `reactions` file into `chemistryProperties`:

In `constant/chemistryProperties`, add:

```
reactions
(
    methaneReaction
    {
        type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
        reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
        A           5.012e11;
        beta        0;
        Ta          24358;
    }
);
```

This way, when `foamChemistryReader` reads `chemistryProperties`, it will find the `reactions` keyword locally and incorporate the reaction mechanism without needing to separately parse the `reactions` file.

## Summary

- **Single change**: Add the `reactions` sub-dictionary containing the `methaneReaction` entry directly into `constant/chemistryProperties`.
- No changes needed to any other files.
</Review_Analysis>
</Attempt>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
Attempt to return primitive entry ITstream : .../constant/chemistryProperties/reactions, lines 28-37...
    primitiveEntry 'reactions' comprises 
        on line 28 the punctuation token '('
        on line 29 the word 'methaneReaction'
        ...
as a sub-dictionary
```

This tells us that the `reactions` entry in `chemistryProperties` is being interpreted as a flat list of primitives (strings, numbers, etc.) rather than a proper sub-dictionary. The problem is the **format of the reactions sub-dictionary**.

Looking at the current `chemistryProperties`, the `reactions` block is written as:

```
reactions
(
    methaneReaction
    {
        type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
        reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
        A           5.012e11;
        beta        0;
        Ta          24358;
    }
);
```

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the `reactions` keyword expects each reaction to be defined as a **named sub-dictionary** where the reaction name is the key. The syntax should be:

```
reactions
{
    methaneReaction
    {
        type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
        reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
        A           5.012e11;
        beta        0;
        Ta          24358;
    }
}
```

The issue is subtle but critical: the parentheses `(` `)` around the reactions list are wrong. In OpenFOAM 10, the reactions sub-dictionary uses **curly braces `{}`**, not parentheses `()`, to define the collection. Parentheses are used for lists of words/tokens, but for named sub-dictionaries like reactions, curly braces are required.

## Root Cause

The `reactions` sub-dictionary in `chemistryProperties` uses parentheses `()` instead of curly braces `{}`. OpenFOAM 10's chemistry reader expects `reactions` to be a dictionary (key-value pairs), not a list, and the syntax must use `{}`.

## Proposed Solution

In `constant/chemistryProperties`, change the `reactions` block from using parentheses to curly braces:

Change:
```
reactions
(
    methaneReaction
    {
        ...
    }
);
```

To:
```
reactions
{
    methaneReaction
    {
        type        irreversibleArrheniusReaction;
        reaction    "CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O";
        A           5.012e11;
        beta        0;
        Ta          24358;
    }
}
```

Everything else in `chemistryProperties` remains unchanged. The key difference is replacing the outer `(` `)` with `{}` to create a proper dictionary structure for the reactions.

## Summary

- **Single change**: In `constant/chemistryProperties`, replace the outer parentheses `(` `)` surrounding the reactions block with curly braces `{}`.
- The inner structure of the `methaneReaction` sub-dictionary stays the same.
- No other files need modification.
</Review_Analysis>
</Attempt>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
cannot be called for a calculatedFvPatchField
    on patch fuel of field p in file ".../0/p"
    You are probably trying to solve for a field with a default boundary condition.
```

This error occurs because the pressure field `p` has `calculated` boundary conditions on the `fuel`, `air`, and `outlet` patches. In OpenFOAM's `reactingFoam` solver, the pressure field is actively solved (via the PIMPLE algorithm), so it requires proper boundary conditions — not `calculated`, which is a passive/derived type that cannot participate in the matrix assembly (specifically, it cannot provide `gradientInternalCoeffs()` needed by the Laplacian operator).

## Root Cause

Looking at the `0/p` file:

```
fuel
{
    type            calculated;
    value           $internalField;
}
air
{
    type            calculated;
    value           $internalField;
}
outlet
{
    type            calculated;
    value           $internalField;
}
```

The `calculated` type is appropriate for derived fields like `alpha.t` (turbulent viscosity) that are computed from other fields, but **pressure is solved directly** in `reactingFoam`. It needs proper boundary conditions like `fixedMeanPressure`, `totalPressure`, or even `zeroGradient` depending on the situation.

## Proposed Solution

Change the boundary conditions for `p` in `0/p` to proper types that can be solved. For a counterflow flame with the outlet acting as a pressure boundary, the typical approach is:

- **fuel** (velocity-inlet-like patch): Use `fixedMeanPressure` with a reference pressure, or alternatively `totalPressure` with `p0 uniform 101325` and `gamma 1.4`. Given the internal field is ~101325 Pa, `fixedMeanPressure` makes sense.

- **air** (velocity-inlet-like patch): Same as fuel — `fixedMeanPressure` or `totalPressure`.

- **outlet**: Use `totalPressure` with `p0 uniform 101325` and `gamma 1.4` to allow outflow while maintaining ambient pressure.

A practical choice would be:

```
fuel
{
    type            totalPressure;
    p0              uniform 101325;
    gamma           1.4;
    value           uniform 101325;
}
air
{
    type            totalPressure;
    p0              uniform 101325;
    gamma           1.4;
    value           uniform 101325;
}
outlet
{
    type            totalPressure;
    p0              uniform 101325;
    gamma           1.4;
    value           uniform 101325;
}
frontAndBack
{
    type            zeroGradient;
}
```

Alternatively, if the goal is to fix pressure at the outlet, `fixedMeanPressure` on the outlet with `pRef uniform 101325` and `fixedMeanPressure` on fuel/air with `value uniform 101325` could also work.

The key point is that **all three non-empty patches** (`fuel`, `air`, `outlet`) need proper solvable BCs — `calculated` is not acceptable for a field that is being solved.

## Summary

- **Change `p` boundary conditions** in `0/p`: Replace `calculated` with proper BC types (`totalPressure` or `fixedMeanPressure`) on `fuel`, `air`, and `outlet` patches.
- Keep `frontAndBack` as `empty` (this is fine).
- Ensure the internal field and values are consistent with atmospheric pressure (~101325 Pa).

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
inconsistent patch and patchField types for 
    patch type empty and patchField type zeroGradient
```

This occurs on the `frontAndBack` patch in `0/p`. The `frontAndBack` patch is defined as `type empty` in `blockMeshDict`, but in the `0/p` file, the boundary condition for `frontAndBack` is set to `zeroGradient`. 

In OpenFOAM, when a patch is declared as `empty` in the mesh definition, the corresponding field boundary condition must also be `empty`. The `zeroGradient` type is incompatible with an `empty` patch because an empty patch represents a completely separate mesh topology (no face connections), whereas `zeroGradient` implies a gradient calculation that still needs to reference the patch faces.

## Root Cause

The mismatch is in `0/p`:
```
frontAndBack
{
    type            zeroGradient;
}
```

While `blockMeshDict` declares:
```
frontAndBack
{
    type empty;
    faces
    (
        (0 3 2 1)
        (4 5 6 7)
    );
}
```

## Proposed Solution

In `0/p`, change the `frontAndBack` boundary condition from `zeroGradient` to `empty`:

```
frontAndBack
{
    type            empty;
}
```

This matches the `empty` type declared in `blockMeshDict` for the `frontAndBack` patch. All four fields (`CH4`, `CO2`, `H2O`, `N2`, `T`, `U`, `Ydefault`, `alphat`, `p`) must have consistent patch types with their corresponding mesh boundary patches. The `empty` type is correctly used for `frontAndBack` in all other field files (as seen in `CH4`, `CO2`, `H2O`, `N2`, `T`, `U`, `Ydefault`, `alphat`), except for `p` which incorrectly uses `zeroGradient`.

## Summary

- **Single change**: In `0/p`, change the `frontAndBack` boundary condition type from `zeroGradient` to `empty`.
- No other files need modification.
